summary:
Basketry — the weaving and coiling of flexible plant materials (reeds, grasses, bark, willow withies) into containers, mats, and other functional objects — is one of the earliest and most widespread human crafts, though the perishability of plant materials means direct evidence is rare in most archaeological contexts. In the Near East, fragments of basketry and impressions of woven materials in hardened clay or plaster provide evidence for basket-making during the Natufian period and the early Neolithic, beginning around 10,000 BCE or earlier.

Baskets served as critical storage and carrying vessels in early agricultural communities, enabling the transport and storage of harvested grain, seeds, and other products. Their production required specialized knowledge of plant materials and weaving techniques, and basketry traditions were likely gendered and familially transmitted skills. The development of basketry technology paralleled and complemented the development of clay pottery — both served broadly similar functions as containers, but baskets were lighter, more portable, and could be made without firing, while pottery offered advantages for cooking and long-term sealed storage. Some of the oldest pottery was apparently made by lining baskets with clay, suggesting a close technological relationship between the two traditions.
